User.title: User
User.description: Represents a user of the AscendFolio application, holding their personal details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used for identification.

firstName.description: The first name of the user.

lastName.description: The last name of the user.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user account was last updated.

Portfolio.title: Portfolio
Portfolio.description: Represents a collection of investment assets owned by a specific user.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Portfolio entity.

userId.description: Reference to the User who owns this portfolio. (Relationship: User 1:N Portfolio)

name.description: The user-defined name for the investment portfolio.

description.description: An optional detailed description provided by the user for this portfolio.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the portfolio was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the portfolio was last updated.

Asset.title: Asset
Asset.description: Represents a specific financial instrument (e.g., stock, ETF, cryptocurrency) available for investment.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Asset entity.

tickerSymbol.description: The unique ticker symbol or cryptocurrency symbol that identifies the asset (e.g., AAPL, BTC).

name.description: The full, common name of the investment asset (e.g., Apple Inc., Bitcoin).

type.description: The category of the asset, such as 'Stock', 'ETF', or 'Cryptocurrency'.

currency.description: The primary currency in which the asset is traded (e.g., USD, EUR, BTC).

exchange.description: The primary exchange where the asset is traded (e.g., NASDAQ, NYSE, Binance). This is optional for some asset types like cryptocurrencies.

description.description: A brief informational description or overview of the asset.

Holding.title: Holding
Holding.description: Represents a specific quantity of an asset held within a user's portfolio.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Holding entity.

portfolioId.description: Reference to the Portfolio that contains this holding. (Relationship: Portfolio 1:N Holding)

assetId.description: Reference to the specific Asset being held. (Relationship: Asset 1:N Holding)

quantity.description: The number of units or shares of the asset currently held in the portfolio.

averagePurchasePrice.description: The calculated average price per unit at which the asset was acquired in the portfolio's currency.

purchaseDate.description: The date on which the asset was acquired.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this holding record was initially created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this holding record was last updated.

definition.description: Stores individual user profile data. Access is restricted to the user whose ID matches `userId` in the path (i.e., `request.auth.uid`).

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, corresponding to `request.auth.uid`.

definition.description: Stores investment portfolios owned by a specific user. Each document includes a denormalized 'userId' field matching the path parameter for authorization independence, allowing direct ownership checks.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this portfolio.

params.name: portfolioId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific investment portfolio.

definition.description: Stores specific asset holdings within a user's portfolio. Each document includes denormalized 'userId' and 'portfolioId' fields for authorization independence, enabling direct ownership and portfolio context checks.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns the parent portfolio and this holding.

params.name: portfolioId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent portfolio containing this holding.

params.name: holdingId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific asset holding.

definition.description: Stores globally available financial asset definitions (e.g., stocks, ETFs, cryptocurrencies). This collection is publicly readable by all authenticated users and fully controllable by administrators.

params.name: assetId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific financial asset.

definition.description: A collection used for Database Access Control (DBAC) to identify administrators. The existence of a document at `/roles_admin/{adminId}` indicates that the user with `adminId` has administrative privileges. Documents typically contain only the ID, serving as a marker.

params.name: adminId
params.description: The unique identifier of a user who holds the administrator role.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for AscendFolio prioritizes security, scalability, and debuggability in alignment with the core design principles and mandates. The design leverages a combination of hierarchical path-based ownership for user-specific data and dedicated top-level collections for global data and administrative roles.

**Authorization Independence:** This is achieved primarily through denormalization. For instance:
*   In the `/users/{userId}/portfolios/{portfolioId}` collection, each `Portfolio` document will include a `userId` field. This allows security rules to verify ownership directly (e.g., `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId`) without requiring a `get()` call to the parent `users/{userId}` document. This enables atomic operations and simplifies rule logic.
*   Similarly, in the `/users/{userId}/portfolios/{portfolioId}/holdings/{holdingId}` collection, each `Holding` document will denormalize both `userId` and `portfolioId`. This ensures that access rules can validate the user's ownership of both the holding and its parent portfolio directly within the `Holding` document, eliminating dependencies on `get()` calls to the `Portfolio` or `User` parents.
This strategy makes rules robust, atomic, and easier to debug as all necessary authorization context is present within the document itself.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** The structure inherently supports secure `list` operations:
*   **User-Owned Data (`/users/{userId}/...`):** By embedding `userId` in the path, `list` operations on `/users/{userId}/portfolios` or `/users/{userId}/portfolios/{portfolioId}/holdings` can be restricted to `request.auth.uid == userId`. This means a user can only list *their own* portfolios and holdings, and the security rules explicitly enforce this scope, preventing unauthorized data leakage through broader queries.
*   **Global Data (`/assets`):** The `/assets` collection is designed for public read access. A `list` operation on this collection will simply allow all reads (`allow list: if true;`), as intended for globally available assets.
*   **Admin Roles (`/roles_admin`):** Access to this collection (primarily for existence checks) is tightly controlled, ensuring that `list` operations (if ever needed by an admin interface) would only return documents if the requester is an admin, or is performing a specific existence check for their own UID. The structure ensures that client-side queries do not need to perform additional filtering, as the rules will define the exact permissible scope.

**Structural Segregation and Access Modeling:**
*   **Private User Data:** `/users/{userId}` for `User` profiles and `/users/{userId}/portfolios/{portfolioId}` for `Portfolio` data, and `/users/{userId}/portfolios/{portfolioId}/holdings/{holdingId}` for `Holding` data. This is a classic hierarchical, user-owned structure, making ownership-based rules very simple and explicit.
*   **Global Shared Data:** `/assets/{assetId}` is a top-level collection for `Asset` definitions, making them publicly readable and globally manageable by administrators. This segregates public/global data from private user data.
*   **DBAC (Database Access Control):** A dedicated collection, `/roles_admin/{adminId}`, is used to mark users with administrative privileges. This allows security rules to check for admin status using simple `exists()` calls, adhering to the DBAC principle and avoiding custom claims.

This structure ensures a clear separation of concerns, simplifies security rule development, and provides a highly debuggable system for AscendFolio.
